mnemonics.title: CHALK Method for Teaching Linear Algebra
mnemonics.content:
C - Clear Board Organization (three sections)
H - Halt for understanding checks
A - Annotation throughout (verbal commentary)
L - Logical flow between concepts
K - Keep the pace balanced

Method breakdown:
C: Structure board into definitions, theorems, examples
H: Pause every 15-20 minutes for questions
A: Explain each step while writing
L: Connect each topic to previous ones
K: Slow for proofs, faster for computations

mnemonics.title: 8-Hour Course Planning Mnemonic
mnemonics.content:
INNER PRODUCT Roadmap:

I - Inner Products & Norms (Hours 1-2)
N - Norm properties & Cauchy-Schwarz (Hours 1-2)
N - Orthogonal & Orthonormal concepts (Hour 2)
E - Gram-Schmidt Process (Hours 3-4)
R - Projections & Orthogonal Decomposition (Hours 3-4)

P - Projections applications (Hours 5-6)
R - QR Factorization derivation (Hours 5-6)
O - Orthogonal least squares (Hours 7)
D - Detailed least squares problems (Hours 7)
U - Unified review & connections (Hour 8)
C - Complete problem-solving session (Hour 8)
T - Testing understanding (Hour 8)

mnemonics.title: Student Engagement Techniques
mnemonics.content:
PAUSE Framework:

P - Pose questions to class (after key steps)
A - Ask for predictions (before revealing next step)
U - Use wait time (3-5 seconds after questions)
S - Solicit student notation (have students work small parts)
E - Encourage note comparison (neighbors compare)

acronyms.expansion: Question-Response-Delay-Prompt: A questioning technique where teacher asks, waits 3-5 seconds, calls on student, then prompts if needed
acronyms.usage: Use during Gram-Schmidt derivation when students must predict the next orthogonalization step
Organize the board throughout Inner Product Spaces teaching for easy reference

acronyms.expansion: Write-Pause-Recap-Reveal: Write key step → pause → verbally recap → reveal next step
acronyms.usage: Use when deriving the projection formula to ensure comprehension of each component

acronyms.expansion: I Do-We Do-You Do: Gradual release of responsibility model for problem-solving
acronyms.usage: Use when working through QR factorization examples: teacher demonstrates, class tries together, individual practice

visualTips.visualization:
Divide board horizontally into three zones:
[TOP: Key Definitions & Theorems]
[MIDDLE: Current Derivation/Work]
[BOTTOM: Previous Results for Reference]
This allows students to see the 'big picture' while following current work

Draw a timeline on side of board:
Hour 1-2: ════○ Foundation
Hour 3-4: ════════○ Building
Hour 5-6: ════════════○ Application
Hour 7-8: ══════════════○ Mastery
Arrows show how each phase connects to next

visualTips.concept: Concept Map for Inner Product Spaces
visualTips.visualization:
Central node: 'Inner Product Space'
Branches to: Norm, Angle, Orthogonality, Projection
Sub-branches: Cauchy-Schwarz, Triangle Inequality, Gram-Schmidt, QR Factorization
This visual shows relationships before diving into details

analogies.analogy: Think of constructing a building: Inner product is the foundation, norm is the height measure, orthogonal vectors are the walls at right angles, projections are the shadows cast, and Gram-Schmidt is the blueprint for creating perpendicular axes from any set of walls.

analogies.concept: Chalk and Talk as Cooking Show
analogies.analogy: The chalk and talk method is like watching a cooking show live: You see each ingredient added in sequence (definitions, theorems), hear the chef explain why (annotation), and understand the final dish (theorem proof) because you witnessed every step.

Least squares is like finding the 'best compromise' friend group position - if everyone wants to meet at their own spot, the meeting point that minimizes total travel distance (sum of squared distances) is like the least squares solution.

The orthogonal projection of a vector onto a subspace is like its shadow on a wall: The shadow (projection) is the 'closest representation' of the original vector within the constraints of the wall (subspace), and the shadow is always perpendicular to the wall.
